content.problemStatement:
You are given a tree GGG with NNN vertices numbered 111 through NNN. It is guaranteed that NNN is even.

For a positive integer kkk, let's define a graph HkH_kHk​ as follows:


HkH_kHk​ has NNN vertices numbered 111 through NNN.
For each edge (u,v)(u, v)(u,v) in GGG, there is an edge (u,v)(u, v)(u,v) in HkH_kHk​ too.
For each pair of vertices (u,v)(u, v)(u,v) in GGG such that their distance is at most kkk, there is an edge (u,v)(u, v)(u,v) in HkH_kHk​.
We call a graph good if its vertices can be split into two sets UUU and VVV satisfying the following:


Each vertex appears in exactly one set; ∣U∣=∣V∣=N/2|U| = |V| = N/2∣U∣=∣V∣=N/2.
Let EEE be the set of edges (u,v)(u, v)(u,v) such that u∈Uu \in Uu∈U and v∈Vv \in Vv∈V. It is possible to reach any vertex from any other vertex using only the edges in EEE.
Your task is to find the minimum value of kkk such that HkH_kHk​ is a good graph, and one possible way to partition vertices of this graph HkH_kHk​ into the sets UUU and VVV defined above. If there are multiple solutions, you may find any one.
constraints: The graph on the input is a tree
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains a single integer N. Each of the next N-1 lines contains two space-separated integers u and v denoting an edge between vertices u and v in G.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print three lines. The first line should contain a single integer — the minimum value of k. The second line should contain N/2 space-separated integers — the numbers of vertices in your set U. The third line should also contain N/2 space-separated integers — the numbers of vertices in your set V.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the minimum value of k is 1. The vertices can be partitioned into two sets {1} and {2}. For the second test case, the minimum value of k is 2. The vertices can be partitioned into two sets {1, 3, 5} and {2, 4, 6}.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a BFS traversal to find the minimum value of k such that Hk is a good graph. We start by initializing the distance of each vertex from the root vertex as 0. Then, we perform a BFS traversal and update the distances of the vertices. We keep track of the maximum distance and use it to find the minimum value of k. Once we have the minimum value of k, we can partition the vertices into two sets U and V based on their distances from the root vertex.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of vertices in the graph. The time complexity is dominated by the BFS traversal.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — where N is the number of vertices in the graph. The space complexity is dominated by the storage of the graph and the distances of the vertices.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
# Uses input() or sys.stdin
# Handles all T test cases
from collections import deque

def bfs(root, graph, dist):
    visited = [False] * len(graph)
    q = deque([root])
    visited[root] = True
    dist[root] = 0
    while q:
        u = q.popleft()
        for v in graph[u]:
            if not visited[v]:
                q.append(v)
                visited[v] = True
                dist[v] = dist[u] + 1

T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    N = int(input())
    graph = [[] for _ in range(N)]
    dist = [0] * N
    for _ in range(N - 1):
        u, v = map(int, input().split())
        u -= 1
        v -= 1
        graph[u].append(v)
        graph[v].append(u)
    bfs(0, graph, dist)
    k = max(dist)
    print(k)
    for i in range(N):
        if dist[i] % 2 == 0:
            print(i + 1, end=" ")
    print()
    for i in range(N):
        if dist[i] % 2 != 0:
            print(i + 1, end=" ")
    print()
